headline: Fish in Rice Fields: A Triple Win Against Disease and Poverty
meta_description: Scientists discover that raising native fish in rice paddies cuts disease by 25%, boosts crop yields, and creates new income for farming families.
summary_short: Researchers in Senegal found that adding native fish to rice fields reduces a parasitic disease affecting 220 million people worldwide while boosting harvests.
body:
In the northern Senegal River basin, where rice farming families face an invisible threat, scientists have discovered an elegant solution hiding in plain sight.

Schistosomiasis—a parasitic disease spread by freshwater snails—infects more than 220 million people globally, with the heaviest burden falling on sub-Saharan Africa. Rice farmers and their children are particularly vulnerable, as the snails thrive in standing water between the rice plants. Even with available medication, reinfection is constant, trapping families in cycles of illness and poverty.

Researchers from Notre Dame tested a different approach: restoring native fish to the rice fields. By introducing African Bonytongue and Nile tilapia—fish species that naturally eat snails or compete with them for food—they observed something remarkable. Across more than 400 households studied, fields with both fish species showed fewer disease-carrying snails.

But the benefits extended far beyond health. Rice yields increased by more than 25%, soil nutrients improved, and farmers gained an additional income source from harvesting the fish—all without feeding them.

"We don't always have to choose between improving human health, increasing food production and protecting the environment," said Jason Rohr, the study's corresponding author. The next challenge: scaling this simple yet powerful technique across every region where schistosomiasis takes hold.
